1:
"And it shall be, when thou art come in unto the land which the LORD thy God giveth thee for an inheritance, and possessest it, and dwellest therein;" The witnesses note the firstfruits law given for when Israel was settled in the promised land. The witnesses note that Gill notes the land of Canaan, on whose borders they now were, just entering in. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the time — come in, possess it, and dwell therein. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the come in unto the land — the promise fulfilled; the possessest it — the settled enjoyment; the dwellest therein — the inheritance present; the thanksgiving the response — the Giver owned in the gift (Deut. 8:10).

The witnesses agree the firstfruits ceremony looked forward to the settled enjoyment of the inheritance: when Israel had "come in," "possessed," and "dwelt" in the land, then they were to bring their firstfruits in thankful acknowledgment; the law thus anticipated the day of fulfilment, when the promise long awaited should be a present possession, and provided that the first response of a settled people be gratitude to the Giver; it teaches that the enjoyment of God's gifts is to issue in thanksgiving, the inheritance received with a heart that owns the Giver in the gift (cf. Deut. 8:10).

So the witnesses make the verse come into the land: The witnesses note the come in unto the land: the promise fulfilled. The witnesses note the possessest it: the settled enjoyment. The witnesses note the dwellest therein: the inheritance present. The witnesses note the thanksgiving the response: the Giver owned in the gift (Deut. 8:10). The expositors observe the come in unto the land: the promise fulfilled. The expositors observe the possessest it: the settled enjoyment. The expositors observe the dwellest therein: the inheritance present. The expositors observe the thanksgiving the response: the Giver owned in the gift (Deut. 8:10). The believer is taught to mark the come in unto the land, the possessest it, the dwellest therein, the thanksgiving the response, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that the firstfruits law was given for when Israel possessed and dwelt in the land — the enjoyment of God's gift to issue in thanksgiving, the Giver owned in the gift (Deut. 8:10).
2:
"That thou shalt take of the first of all the fruit of the earth... and shalt put it in a basket, and shalt go unto the place which the LORD thy God shall choose to place his name there." The witnesses note the firstfruits gathered in a basket and brought to the chosen place. The witnesses note that Gill notes this oblation differed from the sheaf at barley harvest, the wave loaves at Pentecost, and the cake of the first dough. The witnesses note that the firstfruits were put in a basket and brought to the place of God's name. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the first of all fruit — the first and best; the in a basket — gathered and carried; the to the chosen place — where God set his name; the Christ the firstfruits — the first offered to God (1 Cor. 15:20).

The witnesses agree the bringing of the firstfruits to the chosen place was an act of worship and acknowledgment: the first and best of the land's produce, gathered in a basket, was carried to the place where God set his name, owning him the giver of the harvest; the giving of the firstfruits confessed that the whole increase was his and the first portion his due; and the firstfruits throughout point to Christ, "the firstfruits of them that slept" (1 Cor. 15:20), the first and best offered to God, the pledge of the whole harvest to follow.

So the witnesses make the verse bring the firstfruits: The witnesses note the the first of all fruit: the first and best. The witnesses note the in a basket: gathered and carried. The witnesses note the to the chosen place: where God set his name. The witnesses note the Christ the firstfruits: the first offered to God (1 Cor. 15:20). The expositors observe the the first of all fruit: the first and best. The expositors observe the in a basket: gathered and carried. The expositors observe the to the chosen place: where God set his name. The expositors observe the Christ the firstfruits: the first offered to God (1 Cor. 15:20). The believer is taught to mark the the first of all fruit, the in a basket, the to the chosen place, the Christ the firstfruits, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that the firstfruits were brought in a basket to the chosen place — an act of worship owning God the giver, the first and best his due, pointing to Christ the firstfruits (1 Cor. 15:20).
3:
"And thou shalt go unto the priest that shall be in those days, and say unto him, I profess this day unto the LORD thy God, that I am come unto the country which the LORD sware unto our fathers to give us." The witnesses note the worshipper professing before the priest that he had come into the promised land. The witnesses note that Gill notes he went to the priest whose course it was to minister, or, by the Targum, the high priest. The witnesses note that he was to profess that he had come into the land sworn to the fathers. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the go unto the priest — before God's minister; the I profess this day — the spoken confession; the I am come unto the country — the land received; the the oath fulfilled — god's faithfulness owned (Heb. 13:15).

The witnesses agree the offering was joined to a spoken confession of God's faithfulness: the worshipper professed before the priest "I am come unto the country which the LORD sware unto our fathers," owning that the land was God's promised gift, the oath to the fathers now fulfilled; the joining of word to gift makes the firstfruits a confession of faith and gratitude, not a mere ritual; it teaches that worship is to be accompanied by the open acknowledgment of God's faithfulness, the heart confessing with the mouth what God has done (cf. Heb. 13:15, the sacrifice of praise... the fruit of our lips).

So the witnesses make the verse I profess this day: The witnesses note the go unto the priest: before God's minister. The witnesses note the I profess this day: the spoken confession. The witnesses note the I am come unto the country: the land received. The witnesses note the the oath fulfilled: God's faithfulness owned (Heb. 13:15). The expositors observe the go unto the priest: before God's minister. The expositors observe the I profess this day: the spoken confession. The expositors observe the I am come unto the country: the land received. The expositors observe the the oath fulfilled: God's faithfulness owned (Heb. 13:15). The believer is taught to mark the go unto the priest, the I profess this day, the I am come unto the country, the the oath fulfilled, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that the worshipper professed before the priest that he had come into the promised land — the offering joined to a confession of God's faithfulness, worship owning with the mouth what God has done (Heb. 13:15).
4:
"And the priest shall take the basket out of thine hand, and set it down before the altar of the LORD thy God." The witnesses note the priest taking the basket and setting it before the altar — the gift presented to God. The witnesses note that Gill notes the priest waved it, putting his hand under the owner's, the waving toward the corners of the earth an acknowledgment of the Lord as proprietor of all. The witnesses note that the basket was set down before the altar. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the priest take the basket — from the worshipper's hand; the set before the altar — the gift presented; the waved to the corners — owning God proprietor of all; the rendering back his own — the earth is the LORD's (Ps. 24:1).

The witnesses agree the priestly presenting of the basket completed the act of devotion: taken from the worshipper's hand and "set before the altar," the firstfruits were formally offered to God through his minister; Gill notes the waving toward the corners of the earth, an acknowledgment that the LORD is "proprietor" of all — the whole earth and its fulness his; the ceremony teaches that what we bring to God is but rendering back what is already his, the offering an owning of his universal lordship, "the earth is the LORD's, and the fulness thereof" (Ps. 24:1).

So the witnesses make the verse before the altar: The witnesses note the the priest take the basket: from the worshipper's hand. The witnesses note the set before the altar: the gift presented. The witnesses note the waved to the corners: owning God proprietor of all. The witnesses note the rendering back his own: the earth is the LORD's (Ps. 24:1). The expositors observe the the priest take the basket: from the worshipper's hand. The expositors observe the set before the altar: the gift presented. The expositors observe the waved to the corners: owning God proprietor of all. The expositors observe the rendering back his own: the earth is the LORD's (Ps. 24:1). The believer is taught to mark the the priest take the basket, the set before the altar, the waved to the corners, the rendering back his own, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that the priest set the basket before the altar — the firstfruits presented to God, the waving owning his lordship over all, the offering rendering back what is already his (Ps. 24:1).
5:
"And thou shalt speak and say before the LORD thy God, A Syrian ready to perish was my father, and he went down into Egypt... and became there a nation, great, mighty, and populous:" The witnesses note the confession recalling humble origins — Jacob a perishing wanderer, made in Egypt a great nation. The witnesses note that Gill notes he was to speak with a loud voice, answering as to the priest's question, said before the Lord in the tabernacle. The witnesses note that the confession recalled the father's low estate and the great nation God made of him. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a Syrian ready to perish — jacob the homeless wanderer; the went down into Egypt — few and helpless; the became a great nation — by God's hand; the grace magnified — look to the rock whence hewn (Isa. 51:1).

The witnesses agree the confession set the worshipper's thanksgiving against the backdrop of humble and helpless beginnings: "a Syrian ready to perish was my father" — Jacob a homeless, endangered wanderer — who went down few into Egypt and there, by God's hand, "became a nation, great, mighty, and populous"; the recalling of so low a beginning magnifies the grace that raised them, that they might not boast but own all to God; it is the spirit of all true thanksgiving, to remember the pit from which we were taken, "look unto the rock whence ye are hewn" (Isa. 51:1), and to glory only in the God who made us what we are.

So the witnesses make the verse a perishing father: The witnesses note the a Syrian ready to perish: Jacob the homeless wanderer. The witnesses note the went down into Egypt: few and helpless. The witnesses note the became a great nation: by God's hand. The witnesses note the grace magnified: look to the rock whence hewn (Isa. 51:1). The expositors observe the a Syrian ready to perish: Jacob the homeless wanderer. The expositors observe the went down into Egypt: few and helpless. The expositors observe the became a great nation: by God's hand. The expositors observe the grace magnified: look to the rock whence hewn (Isa. 51:1). The believer is taught to mark the a Syrian ready to perish, the went down into Egypt, the became a great nation, the grace magnified, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that the confession recalled the father a perishing wanderer made in Egypt a great nation — humble beginnings magnifying the grace that raised them, glorying only in the God who made them what they were (Isa. 51:1).
6:
"And the Egyptians evil entreated us, and afflicted us, and laid upon us hard bondage:" The witnesses note the confession continuing — the Egyptians' cruel affliction and hard bondage. The witnesses note that Gill notes they ordered the male children killed by the midwives, and by another edict drowned. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the evil entreating, affliction, and hard bondage. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the the Egyptians evil entreated — cruel oppression; the and afflicted us — their misery deep; the hard bondage — enslaved and crushed; the misery recalled — heightening the praise of mercy (Exod. 1:14).

The witnesses agree the confession does not pass over the depth of their misery: the Egyptians "evil entreated" and "afflicted" them with "hard bondage," even to the killing of their infants; the recalling of the cruelty of their oppression makes the deliverance shine the brighter and the gratitude the deeper, that they had been not merely poor but enslaved and crushed; it teaches that the remembrance of past misery heightens the praise of present mercy, and that the redeemed do well to recall the house of bondage from which God brought them out, the bitterness of the affliction setting off the greatness of the salvation (cf. Exod. 1:14).

So the witnesses make the verse hard bondage: The witnesses note the the Egyptians evil entreated: cruel oppression. The witnesses note the and afflicted us: their misery deep. The witnesses note the hard bondage: enslaved and crushed. The witnesses note the misery recalled: heightening the praise of mercy (Exod. 1:14). The expositors observe the the Egyptians evil entreated: cruel oppression. The expositors observe the and afflicted us: their misery deep. The expositors observe the hard bondage: enslaved and crushed. The expositors observe the misery recalled: heightening the praise of mercy (Exod. 1:14). The believer is taught to mark the the Egyptians evil entreated, the and afflicted us, the hard bondage, the misery recalled, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that the confession recalled the Egyptians' cruel affliction and hard bondage — the depth of their misery heightening the praise of their deliverance, the bitterness setting off the greatness of the salvation (Exod. 1:14).
7:
"And when we cried unto the LORD God of our fathers, the LORD heard our voice, and looked on our affliction, and our labour, and our oppression:" The witnesses note the confession — Israel cried, and the LORD heard and looked on their affliction. The witnesses note that Gill notes they cried unto the LORD by reason of their hard bondage. The witnesses note that the LORD heard their voice and looked on their affliction, labour, and oppression. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the we cried unto the LORD — the cry of distress; the the LORD heard — his gracious hearing; the looked on our affliction — not indifferent to their suffering; the prayer the resort — the LORD hears the afflicted (Ps. 34:6).

The witnesses agree the turning-point of the confession is the cry of distress and God's gracious hearing: "we cried unto the LORD... and the LORD heard," looking on their affliction, labour, and oppression; the verse shows the way of deliverance — the cry of the afflicted reaching the ear of God, who is not indifferent to his people's suffering but hears and regards it; it teaches that prayer is the resort of the oppressed and that God hears the cry of his own, "this poor man cried, and the LORD heard him, and saved him out of all his troubles" (Ps. 34:6), his eye upon the affliction of his people.

So the witnesses make the verse the LORD heard: The witnesses note the we cried unto the LORD: the cry of distress. The witnesses note the the LORD heard: his gracious hearing. The witnesses note the looked on our affliction: not indifferent to their suffering. The witnesses note the prayer the resort: the LORD hears the afflicted (Ps. 34:6). The expositors observe the we cried unto the LORD: the cry of distress. The expositors observe the the LORD heard: his gracious hearing. The expositors observe the looked on our affliction: not indifferent to their suffering. The expositors observe the prayer the resort: the LORD hears the afflicted (Ps. 34:6). The believer is taught to mark the we cried unto the LORD, the the LORD heard, the looked on our affliction, the prayer the resort, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that they cried and the LORD heard and looked on their affliction — the cry of the oppressed reaching God, who hears the cry of his own and regards their suffering (Ps. 34:6).
8:
"And the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt with a mighty hand, and with an outstretched arm, and with great terribleness, and with signs, and with wonders:" The witnesses note the confession of the mighty deliverance — God bringing them out with great signs and wonders. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD brought them forth out of Egypt after some time. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the mighty hand and outstretched arm (Deut. 4:34). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brought us out of Egypt — the great redemption; the a mighty hand — by God's own power; the signs and wonders — the mighty acts; the salvation God's alone — according to his mercy (Titus 3:5).

The witnesses agree the heart of the confession is the great redemption: "the LORD brought us forth out of Egypt with a mighty hand, and... great terribleness, and... signs, and... wonders" — the deliverance wrought by God's own power, not by any strength of theirs; the heaping-up of the mighty acts magnifies the Redeemer and roots the thanksgiving in his work alone; it is the pattern of all the redeemed, who confess their salvation to be wholly God's doing, "not by works of righteousness which we have done, but according to his mercy he saved us" (Titus 3:5), the great deliverance his alone to be praised.

So the witnesses make the verse brought us forth: The witnesses note the brought us out of Egypt: the great redemption. The witnesses note the a mighty hand: by God's own power. The witnesses note the signs and wonders: the mighty acts. The witnesses note the salvation God's alone: according to his mercy (Titus 3:5). The expositors observe the brought us out of Egypt: the great redemption. The expositors observe the a mighty hand: by God's own power. The expositors observe the signs and wonders: the mighty acts. The expositors observe the salvation God's alone: according to his mercy (Titus 3:5). The believer is taught to mark the brought us out of Egypt, the a mighty hand, the signs and wonders, the salvation God's alone, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that God brought them out with a mighty hand and great wonders — the redemption wrought by his power alone, the thanksgiving rooted in his work, salvation his alone to be praised (Titus 3:5).
9:
"And he hath brought us into this place, and hath given us this land, even a land that floweth with milk and honey." The witnesses note the confession's close — God brought them to the land of milk and honey. The witnesses note that Gill notes he brought them to this place after forty years' travel through the wilderness. The witnesses note that the land flowed with milk and honey. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the brought us into this place — the journey ended; the given us this land — the rich inheritance; the milk and honey — the plenty of the good land; the out, that he might bring in — redemption completed (Deut. 6:23).

The witnesses agree the confession ends where the firstfruits begin — in the good land God gave: he "brought us into this place, and hath given us this land," the long journey ended in the rich inheritance "flowing with milk and honey"; the bringing-in completes the redemption begun in the bringing-out, the God who saved from Egypt settling them in plenty; it is the comfort of the redeemed, that God who delivers also provides and brings to the promised rest, the firstfruits of the good land a token of his finished mercy, "he brought them out... that he might bring them in" (Deut. 6:23).

So the witnesses make the verse into this land: The witnesses note the brought us into this place: the journey ended. The witnesses note the given us this land: the rich inheritance. The witnesses note the milk and honey: the plenty of the good land. The witnesses note the out, that he might bring in: redemption completed (Deut. 6:23). The expositors observe the brought us into this place: the journey ended. The expositors observe the given us this land: the rich inheritance. The expositors observe the milk and honey: the plenty of the good land. The expositors observe the out, that he might bring in: redemption completed (Deut. 6:23). The believer is taught to mark the brought us into this place, the given us this land, the milk and honey, the out, that he might bring in, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that God brought them into the land of milk and honey — the bringing-in completing the bringing-out, the God who delivers also providing the promised rest, redemption finished (Deut. 6:23).
10:
"And now, behold, I have brought the firstfruits of the land, which thou, O LORD, hast given me. And thou shalt set it before the LORD thy God, and worship before the LORD thy God:" The witnesses note the offering presented and the worshipper bowing before the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes the bringing of the firstfruits. The witnesses note that Wesley identifies "it" as the basket of firstfruits. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the I have brought the firstfruits — the gift presented; the which thou hast given me — all from God; the set it before the LORD — the offering laid down; the worship before the LORD — gratitude in bowed devotion (Ps. 116:12).

The witnesses agree the confession issues in offering and worship: having rehearsed God's mercies, the worshipper presents the firstfruits — "which thou, O LORD, hast given me" — and bows in worship before him; the giving and the adoration flow from the remembrance of grace, the gift an acknowledgment that all came from God and the worship the heart's response; it is the fitting end of all recounting of God's goodness, that gratitude express itself in offering and bowed devotion, "what shall I render unto the LORD for all his benefits?" (Ps. 116:12), the firstfruits and the worship together rendering thanks.

So the witnesses make the verse offer and worship: The witnesses note the I have brought the firstfruits: the gift presented. The witnesses note the which thou hast given me: all from God. The witnesses note the set it before the LORD: the offering laid down. The witnesses note the worship before the LORD: gratitude in bowed devotion (Ps. 116:12). The expositors observe the I have brought the firstfruits: the gift presented. The expositors observe the which thou hast given me: all from God. The expositors observe the set it before the LORD: the offering laid down. The expositors observe the worship before the LORD: gratitude in bowed devotion (Ps. 116:12). The believer is taught to mark the I have brought the firstfruits, the which thou hast given me, the set it before the LORD, the worship before the LORD, and to bring his firstfruits with thanksgiving. The verse thus records that the worshipper presented the firstfruits and bowed before the LORD — the offering and worship flowing from remembered grace, gratitude rendering thanks in gift and devotion (Ps. 116:12).
